coredata.dc:title: Interaction of proteins with lemon-juice/glutathione-derived carbon nanodot: Interplay of induced-aggregation and co-solubilization
coredata.dc:creator: Bihani, Omkar
Rai, Tripti
Panda, Debashis
coredata.dc:description: The accumulation of protein aggregates (tau) causes Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), and a range of neurodegenerative diseases. To develop a less toxic and bio-derived nanomaterials for inhibition of protein-aggregation, carbon nanodot has been used for this study. Nanodot have generated huge interest in biomedical applications owing to unique emission property and good biocompatibility. A carbon nanodot is synthesized from a natural resource-lemon juice and glutathione. The synthesized nanodot possesses excitation-independent emission and nano-sheet like with high graphitic content. Interaction of protein with CND is monitored by intrinsic fluorescence (trp residues), FT-IR and circular dichroism spectroscopy. Whereas it solubilizes the protein aggregates at its higher concentration. Both induced-aggregation and co-solubilization are sequence-independent and dictated by nanodot. The study may shed light on the role of glutathione in glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system toward defence against glycation product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein
Carbon nanodots
Aggregation
Fluorescence
Circular dichroism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic characterization of carbon nanodot

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology , New Delhi

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Rajasthan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board , Department of Science and Technology , New Delhi (DST Project No.: SB/FT/CS-135/2013 ) and the RGIPT-Rae Bareli for financial support. OB thanks Indian Academy of Sciences for Summer Fellowship-2017. Authors thank Akansh Nema and Rakshit Pareek for their help in preparing the Carbon Nandot. TR thanks RGIPT-Rae Bareli for assistantships. Authors also acknowledge Advanced Imaging Centre at IIT Kanpur for Electron Microscopic Characterizations. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: [A] Absorption spectrum of CND (solid, red line) in aqueous solution. [B] Emission spectra of CND in aqueous solution obtained at different excitation wavelengths. [C] TEM image of CND- a single layered nanosheet, [D] Segregated particles in the nanosheet; Inset shows the lattice spacing.
[A] Crystal structure of BSA (PDB: 3V03) [31] and [B] Crystal structure of GOx (PDB: 1CF3) [32], where the position of the tryptophan (labeled) residues are highlighted.

Emission spectra of - [A] BSA incubated buffer (λex =280nm), [B] BSA incubated in 40% ethanolic medium (λex =280nm). CND concentrations- (i) 0μM (blue line), (ii) 7.5μM (olive line), (iii) 24.5μM (green line); (iv) 50μM (red line), (v) BSA in buffer (black line). Inset: BSA incubated in 40% ethanolic media with CND at: a) 0μM, b) 7.5μM, c) 24.5μM, and d) 50μM. Emission spectra of - [C] GOx incubated buffer (left-side: solid lines, λex =280nm), (right-side: dotted lines, λex =430nm), [D] GOx incubated 40% ethanolic medium (λex =280nm). CND concentrations- (i) 0μM (blue line), (ii) 7.5μM (olive line), (iii) 24.5μM (green line); (iv) 50μM (red line), (v) GOx in buffer (black line). Inset: GOx incubated in 40% ethanolic media with CND at: a) 0μM, b) 7.5μM, c) 24.5μM, and d) 50μM.

Intrinsic Tryptophan Fluorescence study at different ethanolic media - [A] BSA (λex =280nm), [B] GOx (λex =280nm). CND concentrations- 0μM (square, blue), 7.5μM (triangle, magenta), 24.5μM (circle, green); 50μM (diamond, red).

[A] ATR FT-IR spectra of- (i) BSA incubated in buffer, (ii) BSA incubated in 40% ethanolic media, (iii) BSA incubated with 50μM CND in 40% ethanolic media. [B] ATR FT-IR spectra of- (i) GOx incubated in buffer, (ii) GOx incubated in 40% ethanolic media, (iii) GOx incubated with 50μM CND in 40% ethanolic media.

[A] Far-UV CD spectra of- (i) BSA incubated in buffer, (ii) BSA incubated in 40% ethanolic media, BSA incubated in 40% ethanolic media with - (iii) 7.5μM CND, (iv) 24.5μM CND and (v) 50μM CND. [B] Far-UV CD spectra of- (i) GOx incubated in buffer, (ii) GOx incubated in 40% ethanolic media, GOx incubated in 40% ethanolic media with - (iii) 7.5μM CND, (iv) 24.5μM CND and (v) 50μM CND.
Schematic representation of methods employed for the synthesis of Carbon nanodots (CNDs).

head.ce:title: Interaction of proteins with lemon-juice/glutathione-derived carbon nanodot: Interplay of induced-aggregation and co-solubilization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Petroleum Technology, (An Institute of National Importance), Jais, Uttar Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Petroleum Technology, (An Institute of National Importance)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accumulation of protein aggregates (tau) causes Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), and a range of neurodegenerative diseases. To develop a less toxic and bio-derived nanomaterials for inhibition of protein-aggregation, carbon nanodot has been used for this study. Nanodot have generated huge interest in biomedical applications owing to unique emission property and good biocompatibility. A carbon nanodot is synthesized from a natural resource-lemon juice and glutathione. The synthesized nanodot possesses excitation-independent emission and nano-sheet like with high graphitic content. Interaction of protein with CND is monitored by intrinsic fluorescence (trp residues), FT-IR and circular dichroism spectroscopy. Whereas it solubilizes the protein aggregates at its higher concentration. Both induced-aggregation and co-solubilization are sequence-independent and dictated by nanodot. The study may shed light on the role of glutathione in glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system toward defence against glycation product.

ce:section.ce:para: The deposition of protein aggregates known as tau leads to a wide range of diseases, like Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and type-II diabetes and other age-related diseases [1–3]. Even the continuous accumulation of such aggregates in the extracellular spaces can cause the organ failure. However, the nature of aggregation is primarily dictated by the amino acid sequence and the physicochemical environment of the protein. In order to decipher the mechanism of protein aggregation in living bodies, in vitro protein aggregation studies are carried out by tinkering its native-state, either by the addition of cosolutes/cosolvents - the salts/surfactants, ethanol and or by varying the physical parameter such as temperature, and pressure [4–6]. Moreover, recent studies implicate coaggregation of proteins during amyloid formation. Different combination of proteins such as bovine serum albumin (BSA) –insulin, lysozyme-insulin, and α-synuclein-Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase have also been selected to understand the effect of cross-seeding [7,8]. Significant efforts have been made to develop materials for the inhibition of protein aggregation. For example, metallic nanoparticles, polymeric nanoparticles, and carbon nanomaterials have been reported to inhibit protein aggregation [9–14]. Nanomaterials-based methods thus emerge as the most suited method on preventive measures for protein aggregation. Therefore, the search for the materials preferably derived from natural resources which are less-toxic in nature for effective manipulation of protein aggregation remains an active area of research.

In recent times, Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have generated profound interest owing to its unique excitation-dependent luminescence and biocompatibility [15,16]. Moreover, CNDs possess all the desired features such as high photostability, good water solubility, and effective surface immobilization, thus emerging as a strong contender for bioimaging and biomedical applications [17,18]. The one-pot green synthesis of highly luminescent CND using lemon juice and a tripeptide, glutathione (GSH) as precursors is employed for this study. The reason for selecting such precursors is that lemon is a natural bioresource which contains weak organic acids as ingredients and glutathione, a tripeptide having sulfur content (-SH group), is often used as a scaffold for the synthesis of nanostructures. The reaction mechanism of nanodot formation is proposed to quite similar to that of glycation product [19,20]. Moreover, glutathione serves central roles in various biological reactions. The glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system has a key role in the defense against glycation product [21,22].

In this study, we have investigated the effects of lemon-juice/glutathione (GSH)-derived Carbon nanodots on the aggregation of glucose oxidase (GOx) and bovine serum albumin (BSA), in order to find out the plausible application of this natural resources-derived material in the pharmaceutical industry and biomedical applications as well. Glucose oxidase from Aspergillus niger is a well-characterized dimeric glycoprotein consisting of 2 equal subunits with a molecular weight of 80kDa each. Each subunit contains one flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) unit. The enzyme (GOx) catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to D-glucono-δ-lactone, and thus extensively used for monitory blood glucose level. On the other hand, BSA, a plasma protein possesses essential physiological role-transport and distribution of compounds in blood and moderately a non-reactive protein. It has a single polypeptide chain consisting of about 583 amino acid residues with molecular weight 66.4kDa. To the best of our understanding, the influence of lemon-juice/glutathione-derived Carbon nanodots on aggregation of either GOx or BSA has not been reported so far. Ethanol is a known protein destabilizer and causes protein denaturation thus promoting aggregation [23]. Here, we have studied the effects of Carbon nanodots on the structure and solubility of glucose oxidase and bovine serum albumin in the buffer and as well as in ethanolic media.

Glucose oxidase (GOx), Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA), Glutathione (GSH) are purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as received. Tris-buffer (SRL), Sodium hydroxide flakes (RANKEM), Ethanol (SRL, HPLC grade), Silica (SDFCL) are obtained from the respective companies. All chemicals used in the experiments of analytical grade and used without any purification. Water from ELGA LAB Water-purifier (18.2mΩ) is used throughout the experiments.

The synthesis of carbon nanodot is carried out using a procedure reported by our group [18]. In a typical synthesis, 30mL of pulp-free lemon juice (Citrus Limon) is centrifuged at 4500rpm for 30min. The supernatant filtered through Whatman paper filter paper (11μM pore size) is centrifuged at 13000rpm for 10min to obtain a clear solution. The solution obtained thereafter is mixed with L-Glutathione at 200:1 (v/w) ratio and sonicated for 20min. The solution thereafter is kept in a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave at 150°C for 120min. The orange brown solution is centrifuged again at 13000rpm for 20min to remove large particles and thereafter eluted with water in a silica loaded column.

A stock solution of protein (either BSA or GOx) is prepared by dissolving the solid protein sample in a 200mM Tris-HCl buffer maintained at pH7.2. The concentrations for the proteins in stock solutions are kept at 3mg/mL.

Protein-alcoholic solutions are obtained by incubating protein in 200mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH7.2) and varied ethanol concentrations, 10%–40% (v/v) at 37°C for a period of 15h keeping the protein concentration at 0.5mg/mL. At 50% ethanol concentrations, the proteins get precipitated. The fluorescence radiative rate constants (k f = φf/τf) is 5.8×107 s−1, which is consistent with the CNDs reported in literature [17,24,25]. By using k f, the extinction coefficient of as-synthesized is estimated to be 6.5×103 M−1 cm−1 at 375nm, which is consistent with the reported extinction coefficient of CNDs. The CND concentrations are kept at 7.5μM, 24.5μM and 50μM. Total volume of each sample prepared is kept at 3mL. Similar conditions were maintained for protein solutions without CND.

Intrinsic fluorescence of protein originates from the presence of the tryptophan (Trp) amino acids, that is monitored to understand the unfolding of the protein native structure. However, aggregation of a globular protein which leads to significant change in the conformation native structure, thereby its Trp fluorescence intensity gets altered. The steady state absorption spectra are collected using a LAB India UV–Vis 3200 spectrophotometer. The absorption spectra are recorded from 200 to 1100nm. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra are recorded on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with a Peltier temperature controller [26]. The emission spectra are recorded at three different excitation wavelengths- 280nm (intrinsic tryptophan residues), 340nm (CND), and 430nm (Flavin adenine dinucleotide, cofactor of GOx). The excitation and emission slit widths are kept at 5–10 for BSA and CND, 5–20 for GOx.

The Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FT-IR (Fourier-Transform Infrared) spectrometer is used to record the FT-IR spectra of the solid samples. The samples are recorded using “attenuated total reflectance” (ATR) mode. The PIKE MIRacle™ single reflection horizontal ATR accessory is used for recording the FT-IR spectra. CNDs are imaged by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (HR-TEM FEI Titan G2 60) mounted with field emission gun FEG TEM at 300kV accelerating voltage [27]. The samples for TEM are prepared on amorphous carbon films supported on a copper//nickel grids.

Circular dichroism data are recorded using Jasco J-815 CD spectrophotometer equipped with peltier temperature controller, in the range of 190–400nm at the rate of 100nm/s with a band width of 1nm [28]. CD spectra are measured using quartz cuvettes. The spectropolarimeter was purged with N2 prior to the experiment. Each CD plot is an average of three accumulated plots and also baseline corrected. The molar ellipticity is calculated from the observed ellipticity θ as 100θ/cl where c is the concentration of the protein solution in molarity and l is the path length of the cell in centimeters. Secondary structural contents of the protein are calculated with the aid of the CAPITO- CD Analysing and Plotting Tool web server [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic characterization of carbon nanodot
ce:section.ce:para: Lemon juice/glutathione-derived carbon nanodot (CND) is synthesized by hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Upon incubation at 150°C for 2h the color of the solution changes to orange-brown from whitish suggesting the formation of CND. The absorption spectrum of synthesized CND in aqueous medium reveals a sharp peak at 283nm and shoulder peak at 342nm (Fig. 1A). The highest intensity transitions are assigned to be a π−π* type, predominantly between the HOMO and LUMO, which supports the observation of π−π* nature for the main absorption band [18,30].

Emission spectra of as-synthesized CND are recorded at the different excitation wavelengths (Fig.1B). The highest fluorescence intensity is found at 360nm excitation, and the corresponding emission peak appears at 442nm. It exhibits a characteristic excitation-independent emission for the excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 to 370nm and beyond that, commonly observed excitation-dependent emission resurfaces. The property of excitation-independent/dependent emission of CND behavior has been attributed to the conformational rigidity and distribution of emissive particles. The synthesized nanodot is highly fluorescent in nature. The synthesized Carbon nanodots are stable in aqueous solution for more than a month at room temperature.

At the molecular level, a typical reaction of amino-group of amino acids and with the carboxyl group of weak organic acid (from lemon juice) leads to the synthesis of CNDs, similar to the formation of glycation products, known as the Maillard reaction. Glycation is the non-enzymatic reaction between reducing sugars, such as glucose, and proteins, lipids or nucleic acids. Formation of carbon nanodot is an intricate molecular process involving complex multistep reactions- a series of rearrangements and cyclization reactions accompanied by dehydration. In the first step, the free amino groups of amino acid residues may react with the carboxyl/carbonyl groups of organic acids/glucose and followed by dehydration forms the Schiff base which is a reversible reaction product. The Schiff base, a highly unstable product reacts irreversibly with amino acid residues of glutathione to form peptide adducts or peptide crosslinks, which may subsequently undergo oxidative and nonoxidative rearrangement, polymerization and other reactions, giving rise to nanodots. The fluorescence from carbon nanodot is proposed to originate from the glycation product - pyrrolopyridine unit (akin to vesperlysine).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is used to characterize the synthesized nanodots. Low magnification TEM image of CND (Fig. 1C) reveals two-dimensional nanosheet (graphene-like) structural features. Moreover, high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1D) show high crystallinity of CND with the appearance of lattice fringes distributed in the nanosheet matrix. It can be noticed from the TEM image that lattice spacing is clearly visible all over the entire range. The lattice spacing (Fig. 1D, Inset) of 0.18nm observed is referred to the [102] facet of graphitic carbon. The CND, therefore, possesses a high-graphitizing structure which originates from a natural source and without any post-synthetic modification.

The fluctuation of fluorescence intensity of the tryptophan residues in a protein directly indicates the alteration in the microenvironment around Trp residues, thereby suggesting the conformational changes in a protein. In general, the unfolding of protein leads to the exposure of buried tryptophan to solvents, resulting in a decrease of Trp fluorescence intensity. Intrinsic fluorescence of BSA, originating from the presence of the tryptophan (Trp) amino acids (Trp 134 & Trp 213), is monitored to understand the unfolding of the protein native structure (Fig. 2 A). On the other hand, the tryptophan fluorescence of glucose oxidase from Aspergillus niger is the result of the contributions of 10 tryptophans per monomer (residues 111, 122, 131, 133, 232, 350, 376, 402, 426, and 503) (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence of tryptophan residues in GOx which are accessible to solvents is greatly quenched.

BSA, incubated in the buffer along with CND at 37°C for 15h shows a considerable decrease in Trp fluorescence intensity upon excitation at 280nm, compared to BSA incubated in the buffer in absence of CND (Fig. 3A). Red shift of emission maxima, about 5nm indicates that the conformational change of BSA triggers the Trp residues to experience the relatively polar environment.

It can be noticed that upon the addition of CND (50μM) the fluorescent property of Trp residues is completely lost, suggesting a major conformational change of BSA. As expected, the emission of CND increases with increase in concentration upon excitation at 340nm (Fig. 3B), the emission peak is centered at 420nm. The conformational change of BSA is therefore induced by CND. Interestingly, BSA, incubated in the 40% ethanolic medium shows a considerable decrease in Trp fluorescence intensity, compared to BSA incubated in buffer (Fig. 3B). However, with the increase in the concentration of CND in 40% ethanolic medium, the emission intensity follows the same decreasing trend as observed in the buffer. Surprisingly, a clearly visible aggregation is observed for BSA incubated in 40% Ethanol at 7.5μM CND and even persists at 24.5μM CND, whereas no visible aggregation is observed in either 50μM CND or in absence of CND (Fig. 3B, Inset).

The fluorescence of GOx, a multi-tryptophan protein is significantly lower than that of BSA, under same incubating condition (i.e. in the buffer at 37oC for 15h). A dose-dependent study of CND on GOx indicates that a gradual decrease of the Trp fluorescence intensity is observed (Fig. 3D). On the other hand, the excitation of incubated GOx (in the buffer) at 430nm yields an emission peak maximum at 525nm, characteristics of GOx bound FAD (flavin adenine dinucleotide). With the increase in the concentration of CND, a blue shift of 20nm for the FAD emission peak is observed, indicative of concomitant burial of the FAD. The presence of tryptophan residues and FAD in GOx provides a ratiometric analysis of fluorescence intensities in order to decipher the structural change. In contrary to observation on BSA, GOx incubated in the 40% ethanolic medium shows a considerable increase in Trp fluorescence intensity compared GOx incubated in buffer (Fig. 3E). However, the emission intensity follows the same decreasing trend with the increase in CND concentration. Similar to the observation on CND-induced BSA aggregation in 40% ethanolic media, a distinct aggregation is also observed for GOx incubated at 7.5μM CND whereas no visible aggregation is observed in either 50μM CND or absence of CND (Fig. 3E, Inset). It should be mentioned here that neither BSA nor GOx exhibits any aggregation behavior in presence and absence of CND incubated in the buffer. It is known that ethanol is a protein destabilizer, thus the addition CND which exists in a state of aggregation promotes the induced-aggregation at the lower concentration. At higher concentration of CND, increased thiol-content (GSH, a precursor used in CND synthesis) in solution helps in solubilizing the aggregated protein. It can be mentioned here that induced-aggregation is protein-structure independent, rather depends on the CND concentrations.

The intrinsic fluorescence studies have also been carried out at different concentrations of ethanol (Fig. 4 ). It can be clearly noticed that in absence of CND, tryptophan fluorescence exhibits two different trends for two proteins with the increase in the concentration of ethanol. For BSA, the intensity decreases whereas it gets increased for GOx, which suggests that interaction of proteins with ethanol is dictated by amino acid sequence. Even at the different volume percent of ethanol, both the proteins exhibit a similar decreasing trend of tryptophan fluorescence with increase in the concentration of nanodot.

Incubated protein samples in presence and absence of CNDs are solidified by evaporating the solvent from the solution by placing the solution in a moderate heat bath. The FT-IR spectra of incubated protein samples are recorded and shown in Fig. 5 . The FT-IR spectrum of BSA alone shows the characteristic bands at 1630cm−1 for the amide I, 1552cm−1 for the amide II and a broadband at 3200cm−1 for the NH stretching of amino acid residues. The above-mentioned stretching frequencies have also appeared for BSA incubated in 40% ethanol. Interestingly, the FT-IR spectrum of BSA incubated with 50μM CND in 40% ethanol is markedly different suggesting the strong interaction between amino acid chains of BSA and CND. It can be noticed that a new FTIR bands appear at 1708cm−1 (ν COO− ), which is the characteristics peak of CND [18].

It has been reported that the nanostructures are prone to adsorb large amphiphatic molecules, protein at their surface owing to high surface free energy (high surface-to-volume ratio). The structure of the protein at adsorbed state plays a central role in the retention of biological activity for plausible applications in biomedical field. To get an insight into the protein-CND interaction, circular dichroism (CD) studies have been carried out. The far-UV CD spectra of incubated protein samples are shown in Fig. 6 . The presence of characteristic double minima for BSA suggests the existence of α-helical structure in the enzyme. BSA incubated in 40% ethanol also exhibits characteristic double minima, which in agreement with the intrinsic tryptophan fluorescence study (Fig. 6A). However, with the increase in the concentration of CND, a red-shift, ~10nm of CD spectra is observed, which may be owing to the involvement of the n-π* transition. The secondary structure of BSA is significantly altered in presence of CND indicating partial denaturation of the enzyme on CND surface. The extent of secondary structural content has been assessed using the CAPITO online server [29]. The far-UV CD spectrum of BSA incubated in the buffer has ∼12% of α-helix, ∼44% β-strand and 53% irregular content. BSA incubated in 40% Ethanolic medium with 50μM CND shows a significant reduction in the α-helix content to 7% with the concurrent increase of β- strand (∼53%).

The characteristic double minima for GOx is also observed suggesting the existence of α-helical structure in the enzyme. GOx incubated in 40% ethanol also exhibits characteristic double minima, indicating no alteration of the secondary structure of the protein (Fig. 6B). Unlike BSA, with the addition of CND the secondary structure of BSA is significantly altered in presence of CND indicating complete denaturation of the enzyme on CND surface. The far-UV CD spectrum of GOx incubated in the buffer has ∼11% of α-helix, ∼45% β-strand and 53% irregular content. GOx incubated with either 24.5μM or 50μM CND shows a complete loss of in the α-helix content to 1% with a concurrent increase of β- strand (∼67%). Therefore, it can be concluded that the structural rigidity of protein which is governed by the amino acid sequence dictates the alteration of the secondary structure of protein while interacting with CNDs.

The higher susceptibilities of proteins to undergo aggregation at a lower concentration of CND may be attributed to their specific interaction with nanosheet. The nanodot which is in a state of aggregation serves as a seed for promoting protein aggregation. The hydrophobic moieties get solvent-exposed and thus alters the intermolecular interaction favoring aggregation. In this study, we haven't observed any induced-aggregation in the buffer, thus ethanol plays a critical role as well. BSA and GOx do not have any similarity in their respective biological functions, hence their sequences are also vastly different. Hence, the driving force for either induced-aggregation or co-solubilization seems to be more dependent on the incubating conditions and less dependent on their sequence identities. It can be mentioned here that the co-solubilization does not disrupt the aggregation behavior.

Carbon nanodot synthesized from lemon juice and glutathione exhibits unique excitation-independent emission. The synthetic mechanism of nanodot is complex in nature and may pass through a series of reactions with similar kind intermediates like Millard reactions. The synthesized carbon nanodot is highly fluorescent in nature, even with high graphitic content. Intrinsic fluorescence (trp residues) intensity of protein gets greatly quenched with the addition of nanodot. The gradual decreasing trend of fluorescence intensity is independent of protein-sequence and ethanol content in the medium. Surprisingly, at a lower concentration, nanodot promotes the protein aggregation. Whereas it solubilizes the aggregates at its higher concentration. Circular dichroism study suggests that the helical structure of proteins are significantly altertred and completely lost for glucose oxidase even at lower concentration of nanodot. The study may help to decipher the role of glutathione in glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system toward defense against glycation product.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board, Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi (DST Project No.: SB/FT/CS-135/2013) and the RGIPT-Rae Bareli for financial support. OB thanks Indian Academy of Sciences for Summer Fellowship-2017. Authors thank Akansh Nema and Rakshit Pareek for their help in preparing the Carbon Nandot. TR thanks RGIPT-Rae Bareli for assistantships. Authors also acknowledge Advanced Imaging Centre at IIT Kanpur for Electron Microscopic Characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.01.211.

Nanoparticle-mediated local and remote manipulation of protein aggregation

Nontoxic carbon dots potently inhibit human insulin fibrillation
Recent advancement of nanostructured carbon for energy applications

Biophysical insight into the anti-amyloidogenic behavior of taurine

An extracellular enzyme synthesizes narrow-sized silver nanoparticles in both water and methanol
CAPITO—a web server-based analysis and plotting tool for circular dichroism data
Origin of excitation dependent fluorescence in carbon nanodots
Structural and immunologic characterization of bovine, horse, and rabbit serum albumins
